Stem: MD simulations compare tensile crazing in glassy linear and nonconcatenated ring polymers as a function of contour length (e.g., \(N_{\rm L}\) and \(N_{\rm R}\)). The tensile stress \(\sigma_z\) is tracked versus stretch \(\lambda\), showing regimes I–III, including a drawing-stress plateau \(S\) during craze growth (regime II) when a stable craze forms. For rings, short contour lengths show pullout and no stable plateau, while sufficiently long rings show a well-developed plateau and ultimate failure involving chain scission, indicated by an increasing fraction of broken bonds \(f_{\text{broken}}\). The paper argues that only a fraction of topological constraints survive craze formation to form an entanglement network, and that network properties (e.g., \(N_e^R\), \(d_e^R\), \(\lambda_{\rm max}^R\)) relate to craze structure and stresses via generalized models.
Q1: What conditional relationships does the text establish between polymer topology/contour length (linear vs ring; \(N_{\rm L}\), \(N_{\rm R}\) relative to \(N_e\) or \(N_e^R\)) and (i) whether a stable craze with a plateau stress \(S\) appears in regime II and (ii) whether regime III failure is dominated by pullout versus chain scission?
Q2: Using only the text’s concepts, explain mechanistically why changing topology from linear to ring can change craze growth and drawing stress without changing interactions, and why only a fraction of the topological constraints (about \(\sim 1/4\) in this work) can act as an entanglement network that supports stable craze formation.
Q3: Propose an operational criterion, based on the described observables \(\sigma_z(\lambda)\) and/or \(f_{\text{broken}}(\lambda)\), to (a) identify the onset of stable craze growth in regime II and (b) determine the failure point \(\lambda_{\rm failure}\) as defined in the text.
Q4: Suggest one additional simulation/observable (restricted to methods mentioned in the text, e.g., introducing open ends or pulling internal chain segments with fixed monomers, or tracking topological constraints) that would test the interpretation that an entanglement network survives craze formation in rings; predict the qualitative difference in the outcome for rings below versus above the stated contour-length threshold for stable crazing.
Q5: What exact numerical value of the prefactor (of order unity) relating \(\Lambda\) to \(\lambda_{\rm max}\) is used in the paper’s generalized relation for ring polymers?
Q6: The discussion highlights an open question: what determines the fraction (about $\sim 1/4$ in this work) of topological constraints that survive craze formation to constitute the entanglement network. Based on the text’s links among $\Lambda_{\rm R}$, $d_e^{\rm R}$, $\langle D_0\rangle^{\rm R}$, and $S$, what competing hypotheses could explain why only a subset survives, and what observable signatures during crazing would distinguish between these hypotheses?
